From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

1935 novel by F. J. Thwaites

The Melody Lingers

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| Author | [F. J. Thwaites](/wiki/F._J._Thwaites "F. J. Thwaites") |
| Language | English |
| Genre | melodrama |
| Publisher | Jackson and O'Sullivan |
| Publication date | 1935 |
| Publication place | Australia |
| Preceded by | *[The Broken Melody](/wiki/The_Broken_Melody_(novel) "The Broken Melody (novel)")* |

***The Melody Lingers*** is a 1935 Australian novel by [F. J. Thwaites](/wiki/F._J._Thwaites "F. J. Thwaites").[[1]](#cite_note-1) It is a sequel to the author's debut novel *[The Broken Melody](/wiki/The_Broken_Melody_(novel) "The Broken Melody (novel)")* (1930).[[2]](#cite_note-broken-2)

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Melody_Lingers&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Plot")]

Dale Jenkins, heir to a rich property in the Riverina area of New South Wales, tries to become a writer.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Dedication

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Melody_Lingers&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Dedication")]

The book was dedicated to Thwaites' great-grandfather "The King of the Riverina" who establishing Buckingbong Station in 1827.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Publication history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Melody_Lingers&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Publication history")]

After the novel's initial publication in Australian in 1935 it was reprinted as follows:

* 1935 New Century Press, Australia[[5]](#cite_note-5)
* 1938 Quality Press, UK[[6]](#cite_note-6)
* 1947 Peter Huston, Australia[[7]](#cite_note-Austlit-7)
* 1950s H. John Edwards, Australia (as part of the publisher's Red-Back Paperback series)[[7]](#cite_note-Austlit-7)

## Adaptation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Melody_Lingers&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Adaptation")]

The novel was adapted for radio in 1936.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Melody_Lingers&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Reception")]

The *Sydney Morning Herald* said "from a purely literary standpoint, the book scarcely calls for criticism, but for renders of a certain type it will doubtless have its own sentimental appeal."[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The *Labor Daily* said it "must enhance the reputation of the popular author."[[10]](#cite_note-10)

The *Sydney Mail* said "The reader will meet some old friends from *The Broken Melody* in the new novel, and may very well enjoy this renewal of acquaintanceship. *The Melody Lingers* is quite up to the standard of this author's earlier novels."[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## References
